query: what are the concurrency models in rust?

results.title: ✨ Google AI Overview
results.snippet:
AI Overview
Rust’s "fearless concurrency" relies primarily on two main models: Multi-threading (OS-level parallelism) and Asynchronous Programming (task-based concurrency). Both are governed by strict, compile-time checks to ensure memory safety without relying on a garbage collector. 
1. Multi-threading (Shared-State & Message Passing)
Rust provides native OS-level threads. Instead of using unsafe global states, Rust controls how data is accessed across threads using two core auto-traits: 
Send
: Indicates that ownership of a type can be safely transferred across thread boundaries.
Sync
: Indicates that it is safe to share references (&T) to a type across multiple threads. 
To manage data safely, Rust uses two distinct paradigms:
Shared-State: Multiple threads access the same data. Rust enforces data safety by preventing unsynchronized mutation, usually by combining a generic pointer with synchronization primitives like 
Mutex<T>
 (for mutual exclusion) and 
Arc<T>
 (Atomically Reference Counted for memory sharing).
Message Passing: Threads communicate by sending messages to one another via Channels (std::sync::mpsc), moving ownership of the data from one thread to another without sharing it. 
2. Asynchronous (Async/Await)
Used for large-scale I/O concurrency (like handling thousands of network connections). Rather than tying a single OS thread to each operation, async Rust multiplexes many logical tasks onto a smaller pool of threads. 
Futures: The core primitive, which represents a value that might not be available yet.
Executors and Runtimes: Runtimes (e.g., Tokio or async-std) manage the polling of these futures, waking them up and running them only when data is ready. 
Why Rust's Model is Different
Rust prevents data races at compile time. The ownership model enforces a "sharing XOR mutation" rule across threads: you can have multiple immutable references to data, or exactly one mutable reference, but never both at the same time. This eliminates race conditions before the code is ever run.
